# Font Handling

Cross-platform font availability, fallbacks, metrics, and emoji rendering.

## Platform Defaults (TkDefaultFont)

| Platform | TkDefaultFont | Size Mode |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| Windows | Segoe UI, size 9 | Points (positive) [27] |
| Linux/X11 | sans-serif (DejaVu Sans or Noto Sans), size -12 | Pixels (negative) [11] |
| macOS | System font (.AppleSystemUIFont) | Points [26] |

Tk defines named fonts in `library/ttk/fonts.tcl`. On X11, sizes are negative
(pixel-based) [11]. Quote from Tk source: "Font size is specified in pixels on X11,
not points. This is theoretically wrong, but in practice works better" [11].

## Font Fallback

When a requested font is not available, Tk **silently falls back** to the platform
default — no error is raised [29].

### Explicit Fallback Strategy

```python
import tkinter.font as tkfont

preferred = ["Segoe UI", "Noto Sans", "DejaVu Sans", "Helvetica"]
available = tkfont.families()
chosen = next((f for f in preferred if f in available), "TkDefaultFont")
```

### Verifying Actual Resolution

`Font.actual()` returns what the system resolved to, which may differ from what was
requested [28]:

```python
font = tkfont.Font(family="Segoe UI", size=10)
actual = font.actual()  # {'family': 'DejaVu Sans', 'size': 10, ...} on Linux
```

## Font Metric Differences

The same font name and point size produces different pixel metrics across platforms
due to:

1. **DPI differences**: Points resolve to different pixel counts [26]
2. **Font engine differences**: Windows (DirectWrite/GDI), Linux (FreeType), macOS
   (Core Text) produce different glyph metrics even for the same font file
3. **Size interpretation**: Positive sizes = points; negative sizes = pixels [11]

### Best Practices

- Use **negative font sizes** (e.g., `-12`) for pixel-exact control across platforms
- Use `Font.measure(text)` and `Font.metrics()` for layout calculations instead of
  hardcoding pixel values [28]
- Modify **TkDefaultFont** rather than setting fonts on individual widgets — all
  widgets using the named font update automatically [26]

```python
default_font = tkfont.nametofont("TkDefaultFont")
default_font.configure(size=11)  # All widgets using TkDefaultFont update
```

## Emoji Rendering

Emoji support in tkinter is **fundamentally limited** [32]:

| Issue | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| BMP limitation | Tcl/Tk historically supports only U+0000-U+FFFF. Characters above BMP (most emoji, U+1F600+) raise `TclError` [32] |
| Windows | Segoe UI Emoji requires Direct2D for color rendering; Tk uses GDI [32] |
| Linux | Noto Color Emoji causes X error in Tcl/Tk scripts [33] |
| macOS | Emoji display can cause "the whole GUI to become black and unresponsive" [32] |

### Workarounds

- Stick to BMP emoji (U+0000-U+FFFF range): checkmarks, arrows, basic symbols
- Use platform-specific emoji fonts (Segoe UI Emoji on Windows, Noto Color Emoji on
  Linux) but expect monochrome rendering only
- Tk 9.0 improves Unicode support but full color emoji remains toolkit-dependent [12]

## Cross-Platform Font Pattern

```python
import platform
import tkinter.font as tkfont

if platform.system() == "Windows":
    _FONT_FAMILY = "Segoe UI"
    _FONT_EMOJI = "Segoe UI Emoji"
else:
    _FONT_FAMILY = "Noto Sans"
    _FONT_EMOJI = "Noto Color Emoji"

# Verify availability at runtime
available = tkfont.families()
if _FONT_FAMILY not in available:
    _FONT_FAMILY = "TkDefaultFont"

FONT_BODY = (_FONT_FAMILY, -13)       # Pixel-based for consistency
FONT_HEADING = (_FONT_FAMILY, -15)
FONT_EMOJI = (_FONT_EMOJI, -16)
```

## Gaps and Limitations

- Full color emoji rendering is not possible in Tk 8.6 on any platform [32]
- Font metric differences between platforms mean pixel-perfect layout is
  not achievable — design for flexibility, not exact positioning
- Fractional scaling on Linux affects font rendering quality
